content:
# Exodus Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34r–34v (PDF pp. 66–67); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 46–47 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_006_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_006_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_006_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh, for by a strong hand he will send them out, and by a strong hand he will drive them out of his land."

**2** And God spoke to Moses and said to him, "I am the LORD.

**3** And I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by My name the LORD I was not known to them.

**4** And I also established My covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their sojourning, in which they sojourned.

**5** And I also have heard the groaning of the sons of Israel, whom the Egyptians are enslaving, and I have remembered My covenant.

**6** Therefore say to the sons of Israel: 'I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will deliver you from their bondage, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great judgments.

**7** And I will take you to Myself for a people, and I will be to you for God, and you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who brings you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.

**8** And I will bring you into the land which I lifted up My hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, and I will give it to you for a possession. I am the LORD.'"

**9** And Moses spoke thus to the sons of Israel, but they did not listen to Moses, on account of shortness of spirit and on account of hard bondage.

**10** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**11** "Go in, speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he send out the sons of Israel from his land."

**12** And Moses spoke before the LORD, saying, "Behold, the sons of Israel have not listened to me; how then shall Pharaoh listen to me? And I am of uncircumcised lips."

**13** And the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron, and He gave them a charge to the sons of Israel and to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the sons of Israel out of the land of Egypt.

**14** These are the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. These are the clans of Reuben.

**15** And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman. These are the clans of Simeon.

**16** And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari. And the years of the life of Levi were one hundred and thirty-seven years.

**17** The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, according to their clans.

**18** And the sons of Kohath: Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel. And the years of the life of Kohath were one hundred and thirty-three years.

**19** And the sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of the Levites according to their generations.

**20** And Amram took Jochebed his father's sister as his wife, and she bore to him Aaron and Moses. And the years of the life of Amram were one hundred and thirty-seven years.

**21** And the sons of Izhar: Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri.

**22** And the sons of Uzziel: Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Sithri.

**23** And Aaron took Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon, as his wife, and she bore to him Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.

**24** And the sons of Korah: Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph. These are the clans of the Korahites.

**25** And Eleazar the son of Aaron took one of the daughters of Putiel as his wife, and she bore to him Phinehas. These are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their clans.

**26** This is the Aaron and the Moses to whom the LORD said, "Bring out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their hosts."

**27** They are the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the sons of Israel from Egypt. This is the Moses and the Aaron.

**28** And it was on the day when the LORD spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,

**29** that the LORD spoke to Moses, saying, "I am the LORD. Speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I am speaking to you."

**30** And Moses said before the LORD, "Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh listen to me?"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~34r (PDF p. 66) contains the continuation from Exodus 5:23 into chapter 6. The parashah petuchah is visible between 5:23 and 6:1, marking a major thematic break. The three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation continues. The genealogical section (vv. 14-25) is written continuously without special formatting, though the clan formulae create natural rhythmic units. The text flows onto folio ~34v (PDF p. 67), completing the chapter and transitioning into chapter 7. Masorah parva annotations appear in the margins throughout, with several notes on the genealogical names and on the divine name formulations. The Codex Vaticanus (pp. 46-47) was consulted as the primary Greek witness. The text of Exodus 6 begins in the lower portion of page 46 (continuing from chapter 5) and extends through page 47. The continuous uncial script preserves the LXX Vorlage, which shows notable expansions and differences from the MT in the genealogy and in the divine speech sections, as noted below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"By a strong hand" (v. 1):** Hebrew beyad chazaqah. This phrase occurs twice in the verse, creating a deliberate ambiguity: is the "strong hand" God's or Pharaoh's? The first occurrence ("by a strong hand he will send them out") most naturally refers to God's compulsion upon Pharaoh, while the second ("by a strong hand he will drive them out") could refer to Pharaoh's own forceful expulsion. The subject of both verbs is Pharaoh (yeshallechem, yegareshem), but the cause is divine coercion. LOGOS preserves the MT syntax, allowing the ambiguity to stand. The LXX reads en cheiri krataia ("with a strong hand"), consistent with the MT.

2. **"El Shaddai" (v. 3):** Hebrew be'El Shaddai. This is one of the most theologically significant verses in the Torah. God declares that the patriarchs knew Him as El Shaddai but not by the name YHWH. The traditional rendering "God Almighty" for Shaddai follows the LXX theos on ("being their God"), but the etymology of Shaddai remains debated (proposals include "the Mountain One," "the Sufficient One," "the Destroyer"). LOGOS retains the Hebrew "El Shaddai" untranslated, as it functions as a proper divine title. The verse does not necessarily mean the patriarchs never heard the name YHWH (cf. Genesis 15:7), but rather that the experiential significance of the name -- as the God who acts to redeem -- was not yet known to them. This is a revelation of character, not merely of a word.

3. **"I was not known to them" (v. 3):** Hebrew lo nodati lahem. The Niphal of y-d-' ("to know, to be known") conveys that God did not make Himself known by this name in its fullest covenantal significance. The question of how to reconcile this with the patriarchal use of YHWH in Genesis has generated extensive discussion. LOGOS translates the clause straightforwardly and addresses the theological complexity in this note. The LXX reads kai to onoma mou kurion oukedelosa autois ("and my name Lord I did not reveal to them"), using deloo ("to make clear, reveal") -- a more explicit verb than the Hebrew "was known."

4. **"The four 'I will' promises" (vv. 6-8):** Hebrew vehotse'ti, vehitsalti, vega'alti, velaqachti -- four consecutive weqatal (converted perfect) first-person verbs that form the theological backbone of the chapter: "I will bring out," "I will deliver," "I will redeem," "I will take." These correspond to the four cups of the Passover Seder in later Jewish tradition. A fifth verb, veheve'ti ("I will bring [you into the land]"), appears in v. 8. LOGOS renders each with "I will" + active verb to preserve the emphatic divine self-commitment. The verbs carry distinct semantic weight: hotsi (Hiphil of y-ts-') = physical extraction from bondage; hitsil (Hiphil of n-ts-l) = deliverance, rescue from danger; ga'al = kinsman-redemption, the act of the go'el; laqach = taking into relationship. The progression moves from liberation to covenant intimacy.

5. **"With an outstretched arm and with great judgments" (v. 6):** Hebrew bizroa netuyah uvishfatim gedolim. The "outstretched arm" (zeroa netuyah) is a standard Deuteronomistic formula for God's mighty acts of deliverance (Deuteronomy 4:34, 5:15, 7:19, 26:8). The "judgments" (shefatim) anticipate the plagues, which are understood not merely as catastrophes but as judicial acts against Egypt and its gods (cf. 12:12, "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments"). LOGOS preserves both terms in their formal equivalents.

6. **"I lifted up My hand" (v. 8):** Hebrew nasa'ti et-yadi. This is the gesture of oath-taking. God swore to the patriarchs with an uplifted hand (cf. Ezekiel 20:5-6, where the same expression is used of the same event). The idiom is functionally equivalent to "I swore," but LOGOS retains the somatic imagery because the physical gesture carries covenantal weight. The LXX renders eis hen exeteina ten cheira mou dounai auten ("into which I stretched out my hand to give it"), preserving the gesture.

7. **"Shortness of spirit" (v. 9):** Hebrew miqqotser ruach. Literally "from shortness of spirit." The noun qotser ("shortness, impatience") combined with ruach ("spirit, breath, wind") creates a vivid psychosomatic image: the people's breath is short, their spirits crushed. This is not mere impatience but existential exhaustion. The word ruach carries both physical (breath) and psychological (spirit) dimensions, and the crushing labor has compressed both. LOGOS preserves the literal "shortness of spirit" rather than paraphrasing as "anguish of spirit" (KJV) or "broken spirit" (NIV). The LXX renders apo tes oligopsuchias ("from faintheartedness"), using a compound that captures the psychic dimension but loses the somatic.

8. **"Uncircumcised lips" (vv. 12, 30):** Hebrew aral sefatayim. This striking metaphor, unique to Exodus, applies the language of circumcision to speech. Just as uncircumcised flesh is "blocked" or "closed," Moses' lips are obstructed. The expression connects to Moses' earlier protest at the burning bush (4:10, "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue") but intensifies it with covenantal vocabulary. The word arel ("uncircumcised") elsewhere describes hearts (Leviticus 26:41, Jeremiah 9:25) and ears (Jeremiah 6:10) that are closed to God's word. LOGOS retains the literal "uncircumcised lips" as it is theologically loaded, not merely a description of a speech impediment. The LXX renders alogos ("without speech, speechless"), losing the circumcision metaphor entirely.

9. **"Heads of their fathers' houses" (v. 14):** Hebrew rashey veyt-avotam. The genealogy of vv. 14-25 interrupts the narrative to establish the lineage of Moses and Aaron within the tribe of Levi. It begins with Reuben and Simeon (the two older brothers of Levi) before focusing on the Levitical line. The purpose is to situate Aaron and Moses in their proper place in the tribal order. The term beyt-avot ("fathers' houses") denotes the patrilineal clan structure that organizes Israelite society. LOGOS renders "fathers' houses" consistently.

10. **"Jochebed his father's sister" (v. 20):** Hebrew Yokheved dodat aviv. The word dodah means "father's sister, aunt." Amram's marriage to his paternal aunt would later be prohibited under the Levitical code (Leviticus 18:12, 20:19), but at this pre-Sinai stage no such prohibition existed. The LXX reads Iochabed ten thugatera tou adelphou tou patros autou ("Jochebed the daughter of his father's brother"), transforming "father's sister" into "father's brother's daughter" (i.e., a cousin), likely to mitigate the later legal difficulty. LOGOS follows the MT.

11. **"This is the Aaron and the Moses" / "This is the Moses and the Aaron" (vv. 26-27):** The reversal of name order between these two verses is deliberate. In v. 26, Aaron is named first because the genealogy has traced his priestly lineage; in v. 27, Moses is named first because the narrative now returns to the commission before Pharaoh. The demonstrative pronoun hu ("this is") in both verses functions to link the genealogical record to the narrative actors: these are the same men, identified by descent, who will confront Pharaoh. LOGOS preserves the name order reversal.

12. **"According to their hosts" (v. 26):** Hebrew al-tsiv'otam. The noun tseva'ot ("hosts, armies, organized divisions") anticipates the military-like organization of the Exodus. Israel will leave Egypt not as a fleeing rabble but as marshaled divisions (cf. 12:41, "all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt"). The same word forms part of the divine title YHWH Tseva'ot ("the LORD of Hosts"). LOGOS renders "hosts" to preserve this connection.

13. **Vv. 28-30 as resumptive repetition:** These final verses repeat the commission and objection of vv. 10-12, forming an inclusio around the genealogy. This literary device (Wiederaufnahme in German scholarship) signals that the genealogy was an intentional insertion: the narrative picks up exactly where it left off. Moses' repeated protest ("I am of uncircumcised lips") at the end of the chapter sets the stage for God's response in 7:1-2, where Aaron is appointed as Moses' spokesman.

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Also "Pharaoh king of Egypt" in vv. 11, 13, 27, 29 |
| YHWH | the LORD | Small-caps convention; represents the Tetragrammaton |
| Elohim | God | v. 2 "God spoke to Moses"; v. 7 "I will be to you for God" |
| El Shaddai | El Shaddai | Retained untranslated; not "God Almighty" |
| sivlot | burdens | Forced labor; recurring from 1:11, 5:4-5; here vv. 6-7 |
| avodah qashah | hard bondage | v. 9; compound of "service/labor" + "hard/harsh" |
| zeroa netuyah | outstretched arm | Deuteronomistic formula; v. 6 |
| shefatim | judgments | Judicial acts against Egypt; v. 6 |
| ga'al | redeem | Kinsman-redeemer vocabulary; v. 6 |
| nasa yad | lifted up [My] hand | Oath gesture; v. 8 |
| qotser ruach | shortness of spirit | Psychosomatic exhaustion; v. 9 |
| aral sefatayim | uncircumcised lips | Covenantal speech metaphor; vv. 12, 30 |
| mishpachot | clans | Tribal subdivision; vv. 14, 15, 17, 19, 24, 25 |
| tseva'ot | hosts | Organized divisions of Israel; v. 26 |
| beyt-avot | fathers' houses | Patrilineal clan structure; v. 14 |

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 6 is generally close to the MT in its narrative portions (vv. 1-13, 26-30) but shows characteristic differences in the genealogy. The LXX genealogy includes additional names and variant spellings for several figures. Most notably, the LXX modifies the relationship of Jochebed to Amram (v. 20, see note above). The divine speeches (vv. 2-8) are faithfully rendered in Greek, though the LXX's use of kurios for YHWH and theos for Elohim somewhat flattens the MT's careful alternation between divine names. The phrase aral sefatayim is rendered alogos in both occurrences (vv. 12, 30), a significant loss of the circumcision metaphor.

--- LOGOS
